ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general toast food product class; no Singapore-specific butter toast entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for butter toast. 'Bread and similar products' (A000N) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559003 refers to toast generically; no Singapore-specific butter toast concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of traditional butter toast at a Singapore kopitiam consists of two slices of toasted white bread spread with butter, weighing approximately 80 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Bread, white, toasted with butter — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for plain white toast and for butter separately. Values for traditional butter toast are derived by combining HPB data for white bread (toasted) with standard butter addition at approximately 10 g butter per 40 g bread slice (two slices = 80 g serving). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for 'Bread, white, commercially prepared, toasted' (NDB 18069) and 'Butter, salted' (NDB 01001) were used to cross-validate per-100g macro ratios. Final per-100g values represent the composite food as served.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — white bread GI reference; University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread, toasted
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of white bread (toasted) at approximately 70 (glucose reference scale). Traditional butter toast is white bread toasted and spread with butter. The addition of butter lowers the effective glycemic response slightly due to fat slowing gastric emptying; however, the GI of the bread component dominates and the composite GI is estimated at 70 consistent with the toasted white bread reference. Glycemic load per 80 g serving calculated as: (70 × 40.8 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 28.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salted butter and bread
hypertension.guidance: Traditional butter toast contains approximately 430 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from salted butter and commercially baked white bread; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one slice and consider unsalted butter alternatives to reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index white bread base with significant refined carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Traditional butter toast has a high GI of approximately 70 and delivers around 40 g of available carbohydrate per 80 g serving, producing a glycemic load of approximately 28.6; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion to one slice, pair with a protein source such as a soft-boiled egg, and consider wholegrain bread alternatives to attenuate postprandial blood glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and trans fat contribution
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 100 g of traditional butter toast provides approximately 8.8 g saturated fat and 0.3 g trans fat from butter, which can raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to one slice per occasion and substitute with a plant-based spread lower in saturated and trans fats.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Traditional Butter Toast is a quintessential Singaporean kopitiam breakfast item consisting of two slices of thinly sliced white bread that are toasted until golden and crisp, then generously spread with cold salted butter. The dish is a staple of Singapore's coffee-shop culture, most famously associated with chains such as Toast Box and Ya Kun Kaya Toast, and traces its roots to Hainanese immigrants who established the kopitiam tradition in the early twentieth century. The bread is typically a soft, fine-crumbed white loaf baked in a Pullman tin, and the contrast between the warm, crunchy exterior and the melting cold butter is the defining textural experience. Traditional butter toast is almost always served alongside a cup of kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea) and a pair of soft-boiled eggs seasoned with soy sauce and white pepper, forming the classic Singapore breakfast set. Nutritionally, a standard two-slice serving (approximately 80 g) provides around 296 kcal, 5.8 g protein, 11.6 g fat, and 40.8 g carbohydrate. The food has a high glycemic index (approximately 70) due to the refined white bread base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The saturated fat content from butter (approximately 8.8 g per 100 g) is clinically relevant for cardiovascular and lipid management. Sodium content is moderate to high at approximately 430 mg per 100 g, relevant for hypertension management. The dish is vegetarian and halal-friendly but contains gluten and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in butter toast Singapore?
Is butter toast suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Traditional Butter Toast' as a composite dish. Values are derived by combining HPB Singapore Foodo data for white bread (toasted) and standard salted butter at a representative ratio, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. The composite approach introduces moderate uncertainty in exact per-100g values, particularly for micronutrients. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database toasted white bread reference, which is a well-established analogous food.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from HPB Singapore Foodo (white bread, toasted) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (butter, salted) at an approximate ratio of 80 g bread to 20 g butter per 100 g composite, reflecting the typical butter-to-bread ratio in a kopitiam serving. 2. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as white bread and butter contain negligible vitamin C, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for both components. 3. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 70 × available carbohydrate 40.8 g per 80 g serving) / 100 = 28.6. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite calculation; these are not -1 sentinel values as defensible estimates are available from approved sources. 5. Halal status is assumed based on standard ingredients (bread and butter); individual restaurant preparation may vary. 6. The dish is associated with Toast Box - Funan as per the field survey source record; nutrient values represent the generic traditional butter toast preparation and not a specific branded product.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed composite nutrient profile from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for white bread (toasted) and salted butter.
Applied University of Sydney GI Database toasted white bread GI reference (70) as analogous estimate.
Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Ensured all per_100g values are numeric with no null or string values.
Documented vitamin_c_mg = 0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three required health conditions.
